speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Madeline Groh Gorman recalls her early childhood in Bovill and her parents background. Her mother was from a French upper class family and her father a commoner. After an 18 years separation, they were married in New York. Her father, Tom Groh, and uncle John ran the ball store for many years. On this day, Mrs. Gorman remembers the Indians who traded at her father's store, her uncle's tricks which almost killed him, and the arson attempts to burn the store.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: She also remembers the tent meeting Chautauqua shows Pat Malone and Pete Olson hanging the Kaiser and their return visit to France. Some of the home remedies for your bad burns and the flu are recalled here.

speaker: Madeleine Groh Gorman
words: Of course, there was a lot of halls there. The. So they went with their families for living. But then the clothing and everything that had to be all replaced and all well, and people gave dances and, potluck dinners, they used to call them socials, where they had box baths. That's it. And then at the time, right after the war of 1913, 1914, when the boys came home, when there was so much flu there again, people went all out to help those who were, sick.

speaker: Madeleine Groh Gorman
words: I know that when dad was sick, terribly sick there, we almost lost him. Well, people would bring in hot soup, and they'd have it fixed in such a manner that Mrs. So-and-so would do this. Mrs. so-and-so do that for the following day. And that was you always had the family had at least one warm meal a day.

speaker: Madeleine Groh Gorman
words: That was for sure. But when dad there again, I wish to God I remember the man's name. The doctor had seen daddy, and, he had told mother that dad was very sick. And to be sure and keep the medicine going the way it's supposed to. So mother said yes, this fella came down to see dad, and he.

speaker: Madeleine Groh Gorman
words: He looked at dad and he looked up. Mom, this is Juliette. He said, you got blankets? Yes. He says, go get me all the blankets you can get. And he said, I want hot boiling water. Mother says, I have that. And he says, do you have any lemons in the house? And mother said, yes, he's all right. He's I want three lemons.

speaker: Madeleine Groh Gorman
words: And and he's told that mother to get, a container that could stand hot boiling water without breaking. And mother got it. Well, he went up to the kitchen with her, and I remember him, you know, juicing the lemons and, he put this lemon in the bowl, put a tiny bit of. I don't know whether it was honey or sugar, but a little bit of sweetener.

speaker: Madeleine Groh Gorman
words: Very little. And he reached in back of his, pulled out a bottle and took the cork off and dumped the whole bottle in it, and then poured a certain amount of hot water. Mr.. It up. And he went back in and he says, Tom, he says, you're a sick man. And he says, I want you to drink this, and I want you to drink all of it.

speaker: Madeleine Groh Gorman
words: And he's going to be hot. And dad said, yes. And so he helped dad up, you know, just sitting position more dead than alive. And, dad did drink the whole thing, and I know I remember to the whiskey. Oh, yes, I was with him. And I remember daddy being extremely white. And all of a sudden he got up here on the the cheekbones, way up high.

speaker: Madeleine Groh Gorman
words: You started getting kind of red. And then I noticed the daddy was beginning, you know, the beads of perspiration. And then I remember him telling a mom, he says, all right, now, Juliette, start packing the blankets. And then mother told me, go up and get out. Marry me. Well, the man told her that she needed help and go get.

speaker: Madeleine Groh Gorman
words: And, I know I went up to after it, Mary. And when I came back and I remember, there was awful stink of sweat like you wouldn't believe. I have never had. I have never smelled that odor before or ever since. But it stink was horrible. And, then I was told to leave, go outside and be very quiet and and, not to come back in until I was called.

speaker: Madeleine Groh Gorman
words: Well, all during this time, they were changing daddy and changing the bed and whatnot, giving a kind of a, I guess after he perspiring for so many hours and they, they gave him kind of a sponge bath, kept him real hot, warm and all that. And when I was permitted to go back in to see my daddy, well, he was in a perfectly clean bed and everything, and, I don't know why.

speaker: Madeleine Groh Gorman
words: I guess, you, I don't remember the windows being open, but I remember that there wasn't such a horrible smell. More that I remembered. But that smell was awful, and the man was still there, and, he did say to me, he says your dad's going to be okay now, madam, you don't have to worry about your dad will be okay.

speaker: Madeleine Groh Gorman
words: And then the next morning when the doctor came in, finally he, he saw dad, and he did. Tom. He says, what did you do? That is is nothing. And he's like, hell you didn't. He said, you did something. And he's. I want to know what you did is because it isn't my medicine that did this and that says I didn't do anything.

speaker: Madeleine Groh Gorman
words: I just followed your directions. And now he is. No, Tom. He's. You can you can confide in me. You can tell me. So dad told him and he says, well, I don't mind telling you, Tom. He's. I've got your death warrant here. He's I've got it right here. Your death certificate right here. He's. I was ready to sign it.

speaker: Madeleine Groh Gorman
words: No, no, that's how close dad was. The doctor had given up hopes and he told mother. He says, I thought when I'd come back that Tom would be dead or dying, but I don't know. He's. I've got the papers. He showed it. I think he's right. But the people in Bovill, as I say, there was food there.

speaker: Madeleine Groh Gorman
words: At least one meal, a hot meal. But I can't remember of not even having, a cold meal at all right now. But that's the way they were now when other people were sick. Well, maybe mother would be designated to do something they knew mother was, kind of helpless in her way, but she was willing to get out and do what she could if there was bandages to be taken care of.

speaker: Madeleine Groh Gorman
words: A mother could do that. She would do that if she. If there was a soup. Why, she could make a soup. She would do that. She was willing to do. And, this I will say about the living. She was great and stuff like that. Just marvelous. You know? But, and Mrs. Fleisher was another one.

speaker: Madeleine Groh Gorman
words: She was just tops. And, Well, those are the old hands now. The younger ones were busy with their family, like Helen Murphy and, Mrs. David Young. Mrs.. David, there was several of them, girls. Agnes is the one that I am referring to mainly. She was, another one that was a very, very good.

speaker: Madeleine Groh Gorman
words: There were several other people, but they were older. And I have forgotten her, their, names, you know, and it was or I had moved away before we did. And so therefore I've forgotten the water right away.

words: She lived in the house, and she's treated more like one of the family, one of the Weber girls. And, So that's why I resent calling her a maid. But, she also knew German. speaker: Madeleine Groh Gorman
words: And of course, tradition wise, for Christmas and Easter, one year, we'd celebrate it and do the traditional things of Germany one year, and the following year it would be French. words: Her mother was, Bavarian French. ... French. So therefore, you see this. So we treated Christmas then as is one one, one one way so that we could, keep the traditions going. speaker: Madeleine Groh Gorman
